sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments, AgriFutures, university horticulture extension, and the Rare Fruit Council and WANATCA archives.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing pomegranates are much the same: full sun, free-draining soil, a hot dry summer to ripen the fruit, and steady water once the fruit has set so it does not split. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: The most widely planted pomegranate in Australia is Wonderful, a Californian variety grown around the world for its large, sweet-tart, deep red fruit and softer seeds, which suits both fresh eating and juice. Beyond it, cultivars differ mostly in seed hardness and in how sweet or tart they are. For eating the arils fresh, lean towards soft-seeded types such as Gulosha Rosavaya, Azerbaijani and Parfianka, whose seeds are tender enough to swallow; harder-seeded sorts such as Kazake store and juice well but are less pleasant by the handful. Skip the dwarf ornamental forms (often sold as Nana) if you want a crop, since they are grown for their flowers and bear little usable fruit. Pick a named cultivar to match what you want to make, then check the in-stock table above for what is available now.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree will crop
sections.body: Pomegranates are self-fruitful, so a single plant sets fruit on its own with no pollinator partner required. Bees and other insects do the pollinating, and a second plant nearby lifts the crop further, because cross-pollination raises fruit set well above what a self-pollinated flower manages on its own. So one tree is enough for a backyard, and a second of any variety is a bonus rather than a need. Each plant also carries two kinds of flower, and the smaller, short-styled ones drop without setting fruit, which is normal and not a fault.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Pomegranates want full sun, at least six hours a day, for good flowering and fruit. They are famously unfussy about soil, thriving on poor, rocky, sandy and even alkaline ground that defeats other fruit trees, as long as it drains freely; they will not do well in waterlogged or very salty spots. Aim for a soil pH of roughly 5.5 to 7. They are deeply drought-hardy once established, but a tree kept short of water crops poorly, so treat that toughness as survival, not a route to good fruit. Container-grown plants can go in through most of the year as long as you keep the water up over their first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Give an established pomegranate a deep soak every week or so through the warm months, and keep the water especially steady from when the fruit sets until harvest, because erratic watering is the main cause of split fruit. A good mulch helps hold that moisture even. Feeding is modest: pomegranates are light to moderate feeders, and too much nitrogen, especially late in the season, drives leafy growth and actually worsens fruit colour and size, so go easy. A yearly feed of compost or aged manure plus a balanced fertiliser in spring is plenty for a backyard tree.

sections.heading: Why pomegranates split, and how to prevent it
sections.body: Fruit splitting is the single most common pomegranate problem. It happens when a tree that has been short of water suddenly gets plenty of it, from rain or heavy irrigation, as the fruit nears ripeness: the juicy interior swells faster than the toughened skin can stretch, and the rind ruptures. A split fruit then invites rot and fruit fly. The fix is even soil moisture: water regularly and deeply rather than in feast-or-famine cycles, mulch to buffer the swings, and once the fruit is ripe, pick promptly and ahead of any forecast rain rather than leaving it hanging. Hot, dry-summer districts split far less than humid ones.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Pomegranates ripen in autumn, roughly March to May across most of the country, earlier in the hottest districts and later where summers are cool. The key thing to know is that they do not ripen after picking: a pomegranate taken early will never sweeten on the bench, so it has to come off the tree fully ripe. Ripe fruit deepens to a rich red, flattens from round to a more squared-off shape, and gives a metallic, tinny ring when you tap it. Leaving fruit to colour up fully on the plant builds the best flavour, but the longer it hangs the more it risks splitting, so harvest as soon as it is ready. Picked ripe, the fruit then keeps for weeks in the fridge.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, cutting-grown pomegranate rather than a seedling. Pomegranates do not come true from seed, so a seedling gives unpredictable fruit and takes longer to bear, while a named plant fruits to type within a few years; cuttings strike so readily that named stock stays inexpensive. Check the label for the cultivar name and whether it is a fruiting type or an ornamental dwarf. Like its fellow dry-summer crops olive and fig, a pomegranate is a long-lived, low-fuss choice for a hot, sunny spot. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long does a pomegranate take to fruit?
faqs.a: A cutting-grown pomegranate usually carries its first fruit about two to three years after planting, and reaches a full crop by around year five or six. A plant raised from seed takes longer and may never fruit to type, which is why named, cutting-grown stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Can I grow a pomegranate in a pot or a small garden?
faqs.a: Yes. Pomegranates take happily to a large pot, which suits a courtyard or balcony, and the compact and dwarf cultivars are bred for container life. Use a free-draining mix and the sunniest position you have, and refresh the top of the mix or pot up every couple of years. In a small garden you can instead keep a full-sized plant clipped as a neat shrub or even a hedge.

faqs.q: How big does a pomegranate get, and how should I prune it?
faqs.a: Left alone a pomegranate grows into a large, suckering shrub or small tree of around three to four metres, though it is easily held smaller. It fruits on short spurs on two and three year old wood, so prune lightly: thin out crowded and dead stems and remove suckers each winter, but resist hard pruning, which strips off the very wood that bears next season's fruit.

faqs.q: Why is my pomegranate flowering but not setting fruit?
faqs.a: A young or newly planted tree often flowers for a year or two before it holds any fruit, so the first answer is patience. Beyond that, the usual culprits are heavy shade, over-feeding with nitrogen (which favours leaves over fruit), or poor pollination, so give it full sun, ease off rich fertiliser, and make sure bees can reach the flowers.

WA.intro: Growing pomegranates in Western Australia. The Mediterranean south-west, with its hot dry summers and mild wet winters, is close to ideal pomegranate country, and the plant has a long, well-documented history in the state.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The south-west of WA gives pomegranates exactly what they want: a long, hot, dry summer to ripen and sweeten the fruit, mild wet winters, and only the low winter chill the plant needs, far less than stone fruit. The dry WA summer also keeps fruit splitting and the fungal rots of humid climates to a minimum. The hotter, drier inland and the north can grow pomegranates too, with summer irrigation to size the fruit and hold off splitting.

sections.heading: Where pomegranates grow in WA
sections.body: Pomegranates have been trialled and grown across the south-west for decades. The former Agriculture WA research station at Medina, on the coastal plain south of Perth, ran a cultivar trial through the 1990s; a collector established an orchard of more than two hundred trees at Yarloop on the Darling Range scarp; and another planted a grove in the Yallingup and Margaret River district in the far south-west. Perth backyards carry heavy-bearing trees, and the warm Wheatbelt and Mid West suit them with a little irrigation.

sections.heading: Varieties proven in WA
sections.body: The Medina trial gave WA gardeners a head start on which cultivars perform here. Gulosha Rosavaya came out best for its balance of sweetness, acidity and looks, with large, deep red, juicy arils; Wonderful was rated the next best, a touch smaller and more acidic; and Azerbaijani is another large, juicy, well-regarded type. All three turn up in nursery stock today. More acidic sorts such as Veles lean towards juice and cordial rather than fresh eating.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA pomegranates ripen through autumn, mostly from about March into May, with the hot summers bringing the crop on a little earlier than the cooler eastern states. Let the fruit colour up fully on the plant, since it will not sweeten once picked, but bring it in promptly once ripe and before any autumn downpour, because a sudden drink of rain on near-ripe fruit is what splits it.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's dry summers spare pomegranates most of the fruit rots that trouble humid regions. Mediterranean fruit fly is established across much of WA, but pomegranate's thick, leathery rind makes it a poor target, and it is not among the host plants DPIRD lists, so it is rarely stung unless the fruit has already split. The pests to watch are aphids, which twist and curl the new shoots (noted in the Medina trial), and birds such as parrots, which learn to raid fruit once it splits open. In the hottest inland sun, exposed fruit can also sunburn.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the WA Organism List, and are usually certified and treated before entry. Many eastern nurseries therefore will not post plants to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, alongside WA's own growers.

faqs.q: Can pomegranates handle Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, pomegranates cope with free-draining Perth sand better than most fruit trees, which is part of their appeal. Improve it with compost and a good mulch, keep the water up over summer so the fruit fills and does not split, and feed lightly because nutrients wash quickly through sand.

faqs.q: Are pomegranates a good waterwise plant for a Perth garden?
faqs.a: They are one of the toughest fruiting plants you can grow here, surviving long dry spells once established and even doubling as a hedge. The catch is that a tree left bone dry will live but barely fruit, so for a real crop give it a deep drink through the fruiting months, even if you let it fend for itself the rest of the year.

QLD.intro: Growing pomegranates in Queensland. Pomegranates want hot dry summers, so in Queensland they belong in the cooler, drier inland and the elevated south, not the humid coast, the subtropics or the tropics.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomegranates dislike humidity, and that is the catch across much of Queensland. Along the warm, wet coast and through the subtropics and tropics, summer humidity and rain at ripening make the fruit split, rot and colour poorly, and disease pressure is high. The plant will grow and flower there, but reliable, good-quality fruit is hard to get. The crop does far better in the dry inland and the cooler, elevated south.

sections.heading: Where pomegranates grow in QLD
sections.body: The dependable areas are dry or high. The granite country of the Granite Belt around Stanthorpe, near 800 metres with cold winters and low summer humidity, is the kind of district that suits pomegranates, and the dry western downs and inland are warmer but just as dry. Closer to Brisbane and along the subtropical coast, pomegranates are best treated as a backyard novelty, grown in the most open, sun-baked, rain-sheltered spot you have.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth ripens pomegranates through autumn, and the warmer districts are among the earliest in the country. Pick the fruit as soon as it rings ripe and colours up, and get ahead of any autumn storm, because in Queensland's humidity a split or rain-soaked pomegranate spoils fast.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Humidity is the heart of the problem. Queensland fruit fly is endemic and uses pomegranate as a host, stinging the fruit so that rot sets in quickly, and split fruit is especially vulnerable. Wet weather at flowering also drives the fungal fruit rots, including alternaria black heart, which rots the inside of the fruit while the outside still looks sound, and botrytis grey mould. All of these ease off in the dry inland.

faqs.q: Can you grow pomegranates in Brisbane or on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: You can grow the plant, and it flowers freely, but dependable fruit is the hard part on the coast, where humid air and summer storms spoil the ripening crop. If you want to try, choose the hottest, most open position, keep the soil moisture even through summer, and pick each fruit the moment it is ripe rather than letting it hang into wet weather.

faqs.q: Would a potted pomegranate do better near the humid coast?
faqs.a: Often, yes. A pot lets you give pomegranates the sharp drainage they like, stand them in the sunniest, most sheltered corner, and move them under the cover of an eave or veranda through the wettest spells near harvest. Expect lighter crops than the same plant would set in a dry inland district.

NSW.intro: Growing pomegranates in New South Wales. NSW grows good pomegranates where summers are hot and dry, which means the inland rather than the humid coast or the cold tablelands.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomegranates are at home in the hot, dry inland of New South Wales, where baking summers ripen the fruit and dry air keeps splitting and rot down. The humid north coast is marginal, with summer humidity and rain spoiling fruit quality, and the cold Southern Tablelands and high country can frost young plants and catch the crop with an early autumn frost. Match the site to the plant: the hotter and drier, the better.

sections.heading: Where pomegranates grow in NSW
sections.body: The warm, dry Riverina and Central West are the natural fit, the same districts that ripen citrus and stone fruit, and pomegranates grow there as a hardy orchard and backyard tree. They are also grown in the Hunter Valley, where a few vineyards and orchards crop them despite the greater humidity. The far north coast and the cold tablelands are the harder ends of the state for them.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Harvest runs through autumn, broadly late March to May, with the hot inland a little ahead of the cooler, higher districts. As everywhere, the fruit must ripen on the plant because it will not improve off it, so colour and the tap test are your guide, and a prompt pick beats the autumn rains that split fruit.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the main insect pest across the warmer and coastal parts of the state and will sting pomegranate fruit, with the damage worst where fruit has split. In the humid Hunter and along the coast, wet weather at flowering brings on the fungal fruit rots, alternaria black heart and botrytis. The dry inland, by contrast, has low disease pressure, which is a big part of why pomegranates do better there.

faqs.q: Are pomegranates frost hardy in the NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: An established, dormant pomegranate shrugs off cold to around minus ten degrees, but young plants are tender and a hard frost in late autumn can damage fruit still on the bush. In the cold tablelands, start with the hardiest you can, plant in the warmest, most sheltered north-facing spot, and protect young trees through their first winters with frost cloth.

faqs.q: Which pomegranate variety should I plant in New South Wales?
faqs.a: For a dependable all-rounder, Wonderful is the safe pick and the most widely sold. If you mainly want to eat the arils fresh, choose a soft-seeded type such as Gulosha Rosavaya or Parfianka. In the hot inland almost any variety crops well; on the cooler or more humid fringes, lean on the earliest-ripening types so the fruit is in before the autumn weather turns.

VIC.intro: Growing pomegranates in Victoria. Victoria's warm, dry inland is genuine pomegranate country, home to the largest grower in the nation, while the cooler south is more of a stretch.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomegranates suit Victoria's warm, dry inland, where hot summers ripen the fruit and the low winter chill the plant needs is easily met. The cooler, wetter south and the coast ripen the crop later and less reliably, and a wet autumn there raises the risk of splitting and rot. Frost is the other watch-point: established plants are hardy, but young ones can be cut back by a sharp frost, and an early autumn frost can spoil fruit still ripening.

sections.heading: Where pomegranates grow in VIC
sections.body: The warm, dry north is the heartland. The Goulburn Valley is home to the country's largest pomegranate orchard, and the Sunraysia district around Mildura and the Murray Valley have the hot, dry summers the crop loves. Central Victoria grows them too. Around Melbourne and through the cool, damp south they are more of an ornamental and backyard curiosity than a dependable cropper.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria picks a little later than the warm north, mainly across autumn from about March to May, and later still in the coolest districts. The rule holds that pomegranates do not ripen after picking, so wait for full colour and the metallic tap, then harvest before the first real autumn rains arrive to split the fruit.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly has pushed into parts of Victoria and is a host risk for pomegranate, so check your own district's fruit fly status. The dry Mallee and northern districts have low fungal disease pressure, while the cooler, wetter south sees more of the flower-infecting fruit rots, botrytis and alternaria, in damp springs and autumns.

faqs.q: Do pomegranates ripen reliably in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Less reliably than in the warm north. Melbourne's milder, damper summers and wetter autumns mean later, lighter crops and more spoilage, so a hot, north-facing, rain-sheltered wall is your best bet, and many gardeners here grow pomegranates as much for the brilliant orange flowers as for the fruit. A pot in a sunny courtyard is another good option.

In the warm inland almost anything crops well, with Wonderful the standard choice and the deep-coloured Midnight Velvet and Red Velvet popular too. Around the cooler south, favour the earliest-ripening types so the fruit matures before the autumn weather closes in.
